brief_document.title: Commit clone cleanup: stop copying the write set ~3x per commit
brief_document.purpose: On the durable write path, the same key/value bytes are cloned roughly three times per commit: the WAL buffer commit path clones the BTreeMap into a Vec (buffered_batch / WalBuffer::commit), normalise_mutations re-clones them again, and every individual write takes a full self.buffer.clone() snapshot for rollback. This is pure CPU/allocation overhead — it does NOT change durability or correctness. Reduce the copying: drain the buffer's BTreeMap into batch_mutate by value/iterator instead of clone-collecting it, and make write rollback target only the single mutated key instead of snapshotting the entire buffer. CPU-only optimisation; behaviour and durability are unchanged.
brief_document.task: Eliminate the redundant per-commit copies of the write set on the shard actor's durable write path. (1) The buffer-to-batch path (buffered_batch / WalBuffer::commit -> batch_mutate -> normalise_mutations) currently clone-collects the BTreeMap into a Vec and then re-clones during normalisation; restructure so the buffered mutations are drained/moved by value (owned iterator) into the mutation pipeline with at most one materialisation. (2) The per-write rollback snapshot (each put/delete/cas/apply-durable clones the ENTIRE WalBuffer so it can roll back on failure) should be replaced with a targeted single-key rollback (remember only the prior entry for the key being mutated, restore just that on failure). Do NOT change observable behaviour, durability, the commit linearization, hashing, or replication. NOTE: some clones are correctness-load-bearing — specifically the buffer snapshot semantics on multi-key apply_durable_batch (all-or-nothing rollback) and any key.to_vec() that crosses the beamr actor/term boundary. Preserve those guarantees; restructure carefully rather than naively deleting clones.

requirements.title: Single-materialisation buffer drain into the mutation pipeline
requirements.spec: THE commit path SHALL move the buffered write set into batch_mutate/normalise by value (drain the BTreeMap / owned iterator) with at most one materialisation, instead of clone-collecting it into a Vec and then re-cloning during normalisation. The resulting committed tree, root hash, and durability SHALL be identical to before.
acceptance: buffered_batch / WalBuffer::commit no longer clone-collects the whole BTreeMap into a Vec only to re-clone it in normalise_mutations; the buffered entries are drained/moved through with one materialisation.
Committed root hashes are byte-identical to main for the same write sequence (covered by existing commit/replication/proptest tests).
No change to durability, fsync ordering, or the commit linearization point.

requirements.title: Targeted single-key rollback instead of full-buffer snapshot
requirements.spec: Per-write failure rollback (put/delete/cas and single-key durable apply) SHALL restore only the prior state of the mutated key rather than cloning the entire WalBuffer up front. Multi-key apply_durable_batch SHALL retain its all-or-nothing rollback semantics.
acceptance: A single put/delete/cas no longer clones the whole buffer for rollback; on failure it restores only the affected key's prior entry (present/absent).
apply_durable_batch keeps all-or-nothing rollback (a mid-batch failure leaves the buffer exactly as before the batch).
Existing failure/rollback tests pass unchanged; a test covers single-key rollback restoring the prior value and absent-key cases.

boundaries: MINIMAL CHANGE ONLY — THIS IS THE HARD BOUNDARY: make ONLY the buffer-drain (R1) and targeted-rollback (R2) edits in the directly-relevant files (the WAL buffer commit path, normalise_mutations, and the shard actor's write/rollback methods). DO NOT split files, reorganise modules, move code between files, extract #[cfg(test)] modules into sibling files, or restructure anything. If a file you touch ALREADY exceeds 500 lines, LEAVE its size as-is — CN1 applies to NEW files you create, NOT to pre-existing large files; you MUST NOT refactor pre-existing files for size. The total diff should be on the order of tens of lines across ~2-4 files, NOT a module reorganisation. A large multi-file restructure is an automatic REJECT.
CPU/allocation-only optimisation: SHALL NOT change observable behaviour, committed root hashes, durability, fsync ordering, the commit linearization, hashing, or replication.

SHALL preserve correctness-load-bearing copies: apply_durable_batch all-or-nothing rollback semantics, and any key.to_vec() that crosses the beamr actor/term boundary (do not pass borrowed data across the boundary).
SHALL NOT introduce unsafe code (crate sets unsafe_code = forbid).
SHALL keep no production unwrap()/expect()/panic!() (crate denies clippy unwrap_used/expect_used/panic).
SHALL keep files under 500 lines (extract submodules if needed).
If a clone cannot be safely removed without risking correctness, KEEP it and note why in the result rather than weakening a guarantee.
verification: cargo fmt --check clean for changed files (rustfmt edition 2024; --config skip_children=true to avoid reformatting unrelated module children).
cargo clippy -p haematite --all-targets -- -D warnings clean (all/pedantic/nursery + unwrap/expect/panic denied).
cargo test -p haematite passes in full (lib ~365 tests + integration + proptest), zero failures — especially commit/replication/recovery/rollback tests.
Committed root hashes unchanged vs main for the same write sequences (the existing history-independence + replication proptests cover this).
A representative before/after micro-measure or reasoning showing reduced allocations/copies per commit (the win is CPU, not latency-dominated by fsync).

constraints.text: No file over 500 lines; extract submodules if approaching the limit.
No panics in production code; unwrap()/expect() only in tests.
mod.rs contains only pub mod / pub use; no logic.
All public types implement Debug; all error types implement std::error::Error.

resolved_context.intention: Audit item 'F' from the haematite hot-path perf review: the write set is copied ~3x per commit (buffered_batch BTreeMap->Vec clone, normalise_mutations re-clone, per-write full-buffer rollback snapshot). Drain by value + targeted single-key rollback. CPU-only, durability/behaviour unchanged. Some clones are load-bearing (apply_durable_batch all-or-nothing; key.to_vec across the actor boundary) — preserve them. See ABLATIVE-STACK-ROADMAP.md and the haematite-perf-audit findings.

provenance.quote: as fast as possible, can't have any embarrassing oversights
